headline:
The room made committees
The strait made leverage
deck: Bürgenstock produced machinery, not a sequencing note. Hormuz produced the harder answer: a permit gate over the barrel route the framework was supposed to keep free.
body: The Sunday split-screen was clean: above Lake Lucerne, the US-Iran process acquired committees, working groups and a summit label; at sea, Iran’s chokepoint posture acquired a permission test, with Reuters reporting that only a single small tanker crossed after Tehran said the Strait of Hormuz was shut. [E1][E5]

Qatar’s account of the “Lake Lucerne Summit” described a first high-level committee meeting with US, Iranian and Pakistani participation and said specialized technical and expert working groups had been established, which makes the output implementation machinery rather than a signed sequencing term. [E1] Switzerland described the talks in operational language, focused on how to translate the agreement into concrete steps, which supports the analytical read that Bürgenstock was what amounts to a compliance audit of the framework, not an official process bearing that label. [E2]

That ceiling was not accidental: Reuters reported that Iran said the Sunday talks would cover implementation only, not the substantive next-phase nuclear issues. [E3] The room therefore could register process, assign lanes and keep the framework alive, but it was structurally unlikely to settle enrichment, sanctions relief, Lebanon enforcement or Hormuz governance in one sitting. [E1][E2][E3]

Vance’s public line moved in the other direction, with AP reporting that he called the day “historic” and said progress had already been made in the preceding hours. [E4] The tension is that “historic progress” was attached to a day whose visible product was process architecture while Iran’s stated focus remained Lebanon and the core disputes stayed live. [E4]

The Strait of Hormuz supplied the harder test because the issue was no longer whether Iran could declare the route closed, but whether normal commercial passage had become conditional on Iranian permission. [E5] Reuters reported that after Iran said the strait was shut, only one small tanker crossed, while Fars cited a military source saying no new permits were being issued. [E5]

The US counter-count matters, but it counts a different object: CENTCOM said 55 merchant ships and more than 17 million barrels of oil moved through Saturday, and stated that Iran does not control the strait and that traffic continues to flow. [E6] That makes the denominator war central to the inference, because a military-reported transit count can coexist with a commercial market that treats the corridor as abnormal, permissioned and legally hazardous. [E5][E6]

The permit system is the mechanism underneath the declaration: Reuters reported that no vessel is permitted without a PGSA passage permit, that standard review takes 48 hours, that each permit covers one transit, that a fixed corridor near Larak Island is mandated, that alternative routes are prohibited and that PGSA-approved insurance is required. [E7] Even if tolls are waived for the 60-day negotiation period, the fee is not the same as the gate; the authority to approve the passage remains the leverage. [E7]

The linkage to Lebanon makes the chokepoint a live bargaining instrument rather than a separate maritime episode: AP reported that Iran’s joint military command tied the closure to the US failure to end the war, with the interim deal meant to stop fighting on all fronts including Lebanon. [E8] The counter-case is real: the room did not collapse, working groups are how fragile frameworks survive, and CENTCOM’s flow figures may mean the closure is more declaration than fact; the test this week is whether the committees produce a written sequencing note before the permit regime hardens into the framework’s first real breach. [E1][E6][E7][E8]

key_numbers.label: Crude moved Saturday by CENTCOM count

art.title: Iran's chokepoint
art.caption: Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz shut while Bürgenstock produced committees; a PGSA permit gate now sits over the barrel route the framework was supposed to keep free, with a mandated corridor near Larak Island and the central channel closed to insured shipping · Map: Sprockett, Escalation Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1

evidence_box.fragment: “Only a single small tanker crossed the strait”
“how to translate this agreement into concrete and operational steps”
“we already made progress in the last several hours”
“Standard review and response time is 48 hours”
